narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 35.50, middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and school deciles from Year 1 (3rd decile) and Year 3 (2nd decile)—and predicted a 61.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 35.50, which, while close to the average of 36.70, pulled the prediction in the opposite direction—toward expecting you to pass—by showing solid standardized testing ability. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 3, below the average of 5.71, further nudged the model toward failure by highlighting early academic struggles compared to most applicants. Overall, your grades placed you in lower deciles than typical successful students, outweighing strengths like your near-average GPA and LSAT in the model's eyes. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 92%) of students, so it didn't sway things much either way. This combination made your profile riskier than the stronger academic trajectories that predict bar passage for most admitted students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors to approval next cycle. Keep pushing—you've got the foundation to turn this around.
